skill.name: nodejs-backend-patterns
skill.description: Build production-ready Node.js backend services with Express and Fastify, including API structure, middleware, authentication, database access, caching, and deployment patterns.
skill.summary: Guide Node.js backend design with practical Express, Fastify, API, middleware, and database patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, import paths, environment-variable examples, and reference URLs in documentation. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found, but one code example builds SQL column names from untrusted update keys and should be treated as a publish warning.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Dynamic SQL Identifier Construction in Example
medium_findings.description: A repository update example derives column names from Object.keys(updates) and interpolates them into the SQL SET clause. Values are parameterized, but unvalidated column names can still create SQL injection or unauthorized-field update risk if request body keys reach this method.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly interpolates field names into SQL from an updates object. The surrounding controller assigns req.body to the update DTO, so runtime validation or a whitelist is needed before this pattern is safe.

low_findings.title: Permissive Network Defaults in Example Code
low_findings.description: The Fastify example allows any CORS origin and binds the server to 0.0.0.0. These are common development defaults, but production guidance should require explicit origins and intentional bind addresses.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show permissive network behavior, but they are documentation examples and not evidence of malicious execution. The risk depends on whether users copy them into production unchanged.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Mostly Come From Documentation Syntax
low_findings.description: External command, filesystem traversal, prototype manipulation, weak crypto, and sensitive-file findings are false positives after context review. The cited areas are Markdown fences, relative imports, Object.setPrototypeOf for Error subclassing, bcrypt or JWT examples, and environment-variable configuration examples rather than executable skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review confirmed these patterns are inside instructional Markdown or ordinary TypeScript examples. No executable script, shell command, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interpolated SQL Column Names
dangerous_patterns.description: The update example constructs a SET clause by interpolating field names. A safer version should whitelist allowed fields before creating SQL identifiers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible and affects SQL identifiers, which cannot be protected by value placeholders. It is a documentation example, so the finding is a warning rather than a block.

content.value_statement: Backend teams need consistent patterns for APIs, middleware, authentication, and data access. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Node.js backend guidance with implementation-ready examples.
actual_capabilities: Designs Express and Fastify service structure for REST APIs and backend services.
Explains controller, service, repository, middleware, and dependency injection patterns.
Provides examples for authentication, authorization, rate limiting, logging, and error handling.
Covers PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis caching, transactions, and connection pooling patterns.
Offers production practices for validation, health checks, graceful shutdown, and monitoring.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not a runnable backend generator.
Some examples require adaptation before use in production systems.
It does not replace security review, load testing, or architecture review.
It focuses on Node.js backend patterns, not frontend or mobile development.

use_cases.title: Plan a New API Service
use_cases.description: Create a clear backend structure with routes, controllers, services, repositories, validation, and error handling.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers starting a Node.js service

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Backend Patterns
use_cases.description: Use consistent middleware, logging, authentication, database, and caching approaches across multiple Node.js services.

use_cases.title: Review Backend Implementation Choices
use_cases.description: Compare an existing service against common production patterns for security, maintainability, and operational readiness.
use_cases.target_user: Developers preparing a backend for launch

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic API Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small backend service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a Node.js REST API for a simple product catalog. Include routes, layers, validation, errors, and database choices.

prompt_templates.title: Add Middleware Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design Express middleware for authentication, authorization, request logging, rate limiting, and global error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Data Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a PostgreSQL repository layer with connection pooling, transactions, validation boundaries, and safe update patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Backend Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Node.js backend architecture for scalability, security, observability, dependency injection, caching, and deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: Design a Node.js API for account management.
output_examples.output: A layered service plan with route groups, controllers, service responsibilities, repository boundaries, validation rules, and error handling flow.

output_examples.input: Improve authentication in an Express backend.
output_examples.output: A review covering token handling, password hashing, middleware flow, role checks, rate limits, refresh tokens, and production secret requirements.

output_examples.input: Prepare a backend for production deployment.
output_examples.output: A readiness checklist for environment configuration, health checks, logging, graceful shutdown, database pooling, caching, and monitoring.

best_practices: Pair generated examples with runtime validation and explicit security checks.
Whitelist database update fields before constructing dynamic queries.
Review CORS, secrets, logging, and bind addresses before production deployment.
anti_patterns: Copying example configuration into production without adapting security settings.
Letting request body keys directly control database column names.
Using this skill for unrelated frontend, data science, or infrastructure tasks.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete backend project?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, examples, and review guidance that an agent can apply while building or editing a project.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Express and Fastify, with related middleware, authentication, database, caching, and API response patterns.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during implementation?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompts and guidance are suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code when working in a Node.js backend repository.

faq.question: Is the content safe to copy directly?
faq.answer: Use it as a starting point. Review validation, CORS, SQL updates, secrets, and production settings before deployment.

faq.question: Does it support database design?
faq.answer: It includes PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis, connection pooling, repository layers, and transaction patterns.

faq.question: What is the main security warning?
faq.answer: One SQL update example interpolates column names from update keys. Use a whitelist before building dynamic SQL identifiers.
